content.full_text:
State of California See: 87100 for back-up correspondence
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance •• Administration •• Executive/Legal •• Enforcement •• Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322 5602         322-5660            322-5901            322-6441            222-6444

March 9, 1981


John Woodhead
City Attorney
City Hall
Riverside, CA  92522                           A-81-03-014

Dear Mr. Woodhead:

I apologize for the delay in responding to a question
you raised during our phone conversation of January 27,
1981. I am providing this advice to you pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).

The facts as I understand them are as follows: A member
of the Riverside City Council is a tenured political science
professor at the University of California at Riverside. You
asked whether there is a potential for a conflict of interest
for this councilmember in either of the following situations:

1. Sometimes the professors at the University
make recommendations as a group to the City Council.
For example, a group of professors recommended that the
council not approve the conversion of certain apartments
to condominiums.

2. The City of Riverside has a contract with the
University regarding an athletic complex whereby the
City and the University share the costs and use of the
complex. In the future, the City Council will be making
decisions regarding the complex including decisions
about the contract with the University, changes in the
Master Plan to accommodate the complex, etc.

Based upon these facts, I conclude that there is no
conflict of interest in either situation.

A conflict of interest exists when a public official
has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision to be
made by the decision-making body of which he or she is a

John Woodhead
March 9, 1981
Page Two

member: Government Code Section 87100.1/ An official has a
financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if
the governmental decision could foreseeably have a material
financial effect on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000).

(b) Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000).

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard
to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior
to the time when the decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management.

Section 87103.

The Political Reform Act specifically excludes from its
definition of "income" "salary and reimbursements for
expenses or per diem received from a state, local or federal
government agency...." Section 82030(b)(2). Inasmuch as
the University of California is deemed to be a state agency
for the purposes of the Act, any salary the councilmember
receives from the University is not "income," and thus the
University is not a "source of income" to him. Accordingly,
the councilmember does not have to refrain from making or
participating in making decisions involving the University
of California when the only connection between the councilmember
and the University is the employment relationship.

This is not to say that this councilmember would never
have to disqualify himself from decisions involving the
University of California. If he has other financial interests
which could be affected by a decision of the City Council,
he might be required to disqualify himself from participating

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code.

John Woodhead
March 9, 1981
Page Three


in the decision just as any other official would be. The
question of disqualification is considered on a case-by-case
basis taking into account the nature of the decision and the
manner in which a financial interest may be affected.

I trust this letter will be helpful to you. Please
feel free to contact me if you wish to discuss this letter
or if you have further questions.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh

Is there a potential for a conflict of interest for this councilmember in either of the following situations: (1) Sometimes the professors at the University make recommendations as a group to the City Council, such as a group of professors recommending that the council not approve the conversion of certain apartments to condominiums; or (2) The City of Riverside has a contract with the University regarding an athletic complex whereby the City and the University share the costs and use of the complex, and in the future, the City Council will be making decisions regarding the complex including decisions about the contract with the University, changes in the Master Plan to accommodate the complex, etc.?
sections.conclusion: Based upon these facts, I conclude that there is no conflict of interest in either situation. The Political Reform Act specifically excludes from its definition of 'income' '[s]alary and reimbursements for expenses or per diem received from a state, local or federal government agency....' Section 82030(b)(2). Inasmuch as the University of California is deemed to be a state agency for the purposes of the Act, any salary the councilmember receives from the University is not 'income,' and thus the University is not a 'source of income' to him. Accordingly, the councilmember does not have to refrain from making or participating in making decisions involving the University of California when the only connection between the councilmember and the University is the employment relationship. This is not to say that this councilmember would never have to disqualify himself from decisions involving the University of California. If he has other financial interests which could be affected by a decision of the City Council, he might be required to disqualify himself from participating in the decision just as any other official would be.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City Council member who is a tenured professor at UC Riverside faces conflicts of interest when professors collectively petition the council or when the council must decide on matters involving a shared athletic facility and contract with the University.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest exists because UC employment salary is excluded from the definition of 'income' under the Political Reform Act, as the University is a state agency. However, disqualification may be required on a case-by-case basis if the councilmember has other financial interests that could be affected by a council decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question, analysis, and conclusion sections. Document provided in response to January 27, 1981 phone conversation. Formal FPPC legal advice from Counsel Diane Mauta Fishburn. High confidence extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A member of the Riverside City Council is a tenured political science professor at the University of California at Riverside. Is there a potential for a conflict of interest for this councilmember in either of the following situations: (1) Sometimes the professors at the University make recommendations as a group to the City Council, such as a group of professors recommending that the council not approve the conversion of certain apartments to condominiums; or (2) The City of Riverside has a contract with the University regarding an athletic complex whereby the City and the University share the costs and use of the complex, and in the future, the City Council will be making decisions regarding the complex including decisions about the contract with the University, changes in the Master Plan to accommodate the complex, etc.?

CONCLUSION: Based upon these facts, I conclude that there is no conflict of interest in either situation. The Political Reform Act specifically excludes from its definition of 'income' '[s]alary and reimbursements for expenses or per diem received from a state, local or federal government agency....' Section 82030(b)(2). Inasmuch as the University of California is deemed to be a state agency for the purposes of the Act, any salary the councilmember receives from the University is not 'income,' and thus the University is not a 'source of income' to him. Accordingly, the councilmember does not have to refrain from making or participating in making decisions involving the University of California when the only connection between the councilmember and the University is the employment relationship. This is not to say that this councilmember would never have to disqualify himself from decisions involving the University of California. If he has other financial interests which could be affected by a decision of the City Council, he might be required to disqualify himself from participating in the decision just as any other official would be.

embedding.summary: FPPC counsel advises that a UC Riverside professor serving on the Riverside City Council faces no conflict of interest regarding council decisions involving university recommendations or a shared athletic complex, because UC employment income is excluded from conflict-of-interest analysis as a state agency.
